metadata.split_role: Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, chalciporoid, and early oddball bolete records; first half of former mushrooms-boletes-other.json.
metadata.notes: Split from former mushrooms-boletes-other.json in v4.2.90 to reduce large-file handling/static. Record bodies and order were preserved; each canonical slug still appears in exactly one base mushroom split file. Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file. v4.2.81 added species-specific bolete notes/source links and simplified review reasons for selected records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target in this guide; included mainly for identification and distribution review.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Distinctive mossy-hemlock bolete with a dark reddish-brown cap, yellow non-bruising pores, and a dark streaked/reticulate stem; its fit for Upper Michigan should be checked carefully.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Fall; western source material emphasizes PNW hemlock forests.
research_notes: Look for a granular to felty dark reddish-brown cap, yellow pore surface that does not bruise blue, and a dark brown to reddish-brown stem with paler streaks or coarse reticulation near the top. Often tied to mossy, decayed hemlock wood in western sources, so do not assume this is a routine U.P. bolete.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

Often tied to mossy, decayed hemlock wood in western sources, so do not assume this is a routine U.P. bolete.

staining: Usually not staining; occasionally weak blue bruising on pores
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; often July through October in the Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.

records.edibility_detail: Best approached as an edible-with-caution species: confirm pine association, very long ridged stem, yellow pores, and lack of dangerous red-pored traits.
records.field_identification: Mycorrhizal with pines. Look for a reddish-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface, very long ridged to nearly reticulate stem, prominent white basal mycelium, and a cap margin that can project beyond the pore surface.

records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Dry, smooth to slightly wrinkled cap; yellow to greenish-yellow pore surface that usually does not bruise; yellowish stem with reddish colorations and tiny dots; flesh may stain bluish in some collections. Reported under hardwoods in eastern North America.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert Boletus key: Aureoboletus roxanae
use_links.notes: Hardwood association, yellow/greenish pores, dotted stem, and non-bruising pore surface.

records.edibility_notes: Yellow-pored, dotted-stem Aureoboletus/Xerocomus-type bolete. Do not infer kitchen use from nearby bolete relatives.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food target; useful mainly as an identification species among cracked-cap and pitted/ridged-spored boletes.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Odd cracked-cap bolete with a brown cap, yellow pores that slowly blue, scaber-like stem texture, and a habit of appearing near decayed oak wood.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Check the brown to dark-brown cap that may crack, yellow pores that slowly stain blue then brown, and a stem with brown scaber-like dots or hairy/scaly patches. Often found near or from well-decayed oak wood, which is unusual for a bolete.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Reddish cracked-cap bolete with yellow pores that bruise blue and red-toned flesh in the stem; described from Michigan, but still a specialist ID species.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; originally described from Michigan.
research_notes: Look for a brick-red to reddish cracked cap with pale flesh showing in the cracks, yellow pores that bruise blue then brown, and flesh that is yellow in the cap but reddish in the stem. Microscopic ridged spores separate it from look-alikes.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Yellow wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus with bluing flesh; keep this conservative because names in this group are tangled and wood/fungus associations matter.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer to fall where recorded.
research_notes: Expect a yellow to yellow-brown bolete on or near pine wood/sawdust or associated wood-decay situations, yellow pores/stem, yellow basal mycelium, and flesh that can bruise blue. Compare carefully with B. hemichrysus and B. lignicola.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Peppery reddish-pored bolete like C. piperatus, but with blue bruising and an olive spore print; originally described from Michigan.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; described from Michigan.
research_notes: Small drab pinkish-tan to reddish-brown bolete with reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a peppery/bitter taste. Unlike C. piperatus, it can bruise blue and has an olive spore print.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Classic peppery bolete: dull reddish-brown cap, reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a sharp peppery taste.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; more common in northern/western North America.
research_notes: Look for a small drab reddish-brown to pinkish-tan bolete with brownish-red pores that darken but do not blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a very peppery taste. Usually under conifers but not exclusively.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small red-pored Chalciporus with yellow flesh and pinkish-red pores; type material was collected in Michigan, but use a conservative ID standard.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July to September in reported North American material.
research_notes: Look for a small yellow to red-pink cap, red-pink pores fading yellow-orange/brown, yellow flesh that does not blue, and a yellow-based pinkish stem. Best treated as a specialist Chalciporus ID.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small purple-red/red-pored bolete; useful as a comparison species among peppery/red-pored Chalciporus rather than a major edible.
research_notes: Small convex cap often purple-red to reddish brown, red to pinkish pore surface, yellow flesh that generally does not blue, and a bare stem. Compare with C. pseudorubinellus and C. piperatus.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Pretty and distinctive bolete with pinkish cap, pink scabers on the stem, and a chrome-yellow stem base.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; widely distributed east of the Great Plains.
research_notes: The key combination is pinkish to tan cap, creamy pores becoming pinkish/brownish, pinkish scabers on the stem, and a bright chrome-yellow stem base/basal mycelium. Often formerly listed as Tylopilus or Leccinum chromapes.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

records.edibility_detail: Edible with correct ID and fresh condition, but not prime.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body; microscopy-only images intentionally avoided.

records.culinary_uses: Not highlighted as a prime edible; useful mainly as a recognizable yellow-pored bolete.

mushroom_profile.summary: Clustered brown bolete with a dry brown cap, bright yellow non-bruising pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem often swollen above a short root.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Look for clustered growth, dry brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pores that do not bruise blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a stem swollen near the base before tapering into a short root.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

records.edibility_detail: Use as an ID/comparison species unless local edible-use confidence is stronger.
